title: Ultrastructural studies of neuronal correlates of the pubertal reaugmentation of hypothalamic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) release in the rhesus monkey (Macaca mulatta)
abstract:
Ultrastructural Studies of Neuronal
Correlates of the Pubertal
Reaugmentation of Hypothalamic
Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone
(GnRH) Release in the Rhesus Monkey
(Macaca mulatta)
AYESH D. PERERA AND TONY M. PLANT*
Department of Cell Biology and Physiology,
University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania 15261
ABSTRACT
This study tested the hypothesis that puberty in primates is triggered by a remodeling of
synaptic inputs and/or glial coverage of hypothalamic gonadotropin releasing hormone
(GnRH) neurons. Male rhesus monkeys were prepubertally castrated at 16 months of age and
were killed and perfused either 1 month later (n 5 4, juvenile group) or at 30 months of age,
shortly after initiation of the pubertal increase in pulsatile GnRH release (n 5 4, adult group).
Hypothalami were sectioned, immunocytochemically stained for GnRH, and processed for
electron microscopy. Cross-sectional proﬁles of 77 GnRH cells from the medial basal
hypothalamus (MBH) and the region of the organum vasculosum of the lamina terminalis
(OVLT) were compared between the two developmental stages. GnRH cell and nucleolus size
in the two groups were the same. The percentage of GnRH perikaryal membrane occupied by
synaptic density in the MBH of juveniles was signiﬁcantly greater (P , 0.05) than that of
adults. Differences in the percentage of GnRH perikaryal membrane occupied by synaptic
density were not observed in the OVLT nor on GnRH dendrites in either brain region.
Qualitative analysis, based on synaptic vesicle shape, failed to reveal developmental
differences in putatively excitatory or inhibitory synapses on GnRH cells. The degree of glial
ensheathment of GnRH neurons did not change signiﬁcantly during the two developmental
stages. These ﬁndings provide ultrastructural evidence for the view that, in primates,
neuronal plasticity, and speciﬁcally a decrease in synaptic input to GnRH perikarya, may
underlie the initiation of the pubertal mode of release of this neuropeptide, and therefore, the
onset of puberty in these species. J. Comp. Neurol. 385:71–82, 1997.
r 1997 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Indexing terms: sexual development; primates; plasticity; synapse; glia
In adulthood, the drive by the central nervous system to
the pituitary-gonadal axis is provided by the intermittent
release of the hypothalamic peptide, gonadotropin releas-
ing hormone (GnRH), into the primary plexus of the
hypothalamo-hypophysial portal vasculature. The GnRH
neurons, which number approximately 2,000 in monkey
(Silverman et al., 1982), are diffusely distributed through-
out the anterior hypothalamus and medial-basal hypothala-
mus (MBH; Goldsmith et al., 1983; Silverman et al., 1994).
At the level of the pituitary gonadotrope, the hypothalamic
signal of pulsatile GnRH elicits a corresponding pattern of
luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hor-
mone (FSH) secretion, which, in turn, regulates gametogen-
esis and gonadal steroidogenesis. Interestingly, in higher
primates, the neuronal network governing pulsatile GnRH
release appears to attain maturity during fetal develop-
ment, as reﬂected by pulsatile patterns of gonadotropin
secretion in the fetus (Jaffe, 1989) and infant (Plant, 1986).
Shortly after birth, however, there is a dramatic attenua-
Grant Sponsor: NIH; Grant number: HD13254, HD08610.
*Correspondence to: Dr. Tony M. Plant, Department of Cell Biology and
Physiology, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, S330 Biomedical
Science Tower, Pittsburgh, PA 15261. E-mail: plant1@vms.cis.pitt.edu
Received 14 March 1996; Revised 28 February 1997; Accepted 26 March
1997
THE JOURNAL OF COMPARATIVE NEUROLOGY 385:71–82 (1997)
r 1997 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 tion in episodic GnRH release, which leads to the pro-
longed period of pituitary-gonadal quiescence characteris-
tic of prepubertal development in primates. The onset of
puberty in these species may therefore be viewed as the
result of a reaugmentation of pulsatile GnRH secretion
(see Plant, 1994, review).
We have recently proposed that the pubertal reaugmen-
tation in GnRH secretion in primates is the result of
morphological changes in the neuronal network governing
the pulsatile release of this hypothalamic peptide (Perera
and Plant, 1992). Consistent with this hypothesis is the
ﬁnding that, in the monkey, the cell adhesion molecule,
polysialic acid neural cell adhesion molecule (PSA-
NCAM), a glycoprotein that is thought to be a marker for
neuronal plasticity, is expressed postnatally in regions of
the hypothalamus that contain GnRH cell bodies and axon
terminals (Perera et al., 1993). Moreover, plastic changes
in the GnRH system, in response to castration and steroid
replacement, have been demonstrated in the primate
hypothalamus (Witkin et al., 1991). In addition, in non-
primate species, morphological changes within the GnRH
system have been previously documented to accompany
puberty (Witkin and Romero, 1995), seasonal breeding
(Karsch et al., 1987), and reproductive senescence (Witkin,
1992).
In the present study, the hypothesis that the pubertal
reaugmentation of pulsatile GnRH secretion and the sub-
sequent onset of adulthood in primates is occasioned by a
remodeling of synaptic input and/or glial ensheathment
onto GnRH cell bodies and dendrites was examined. The
rhesus monkey was used as an experimental paradigm,
and the male was studied because the intensity of the
prepubertal suppression in GnRH release is greater than
that found in the female (Plant, 1985; Pohl et al., 1995). It
was reasoned, therefore, that if a morphological correlate
of the pubertal reaugmentation of pulsatile GnRH release
is present, it would be correspondingly most striking in the
male. Thus, an anatomical investigation employing immu-
nocytochemistry and electron microscopy (EM) was per-
formed to compare synaptic input and glial ensheathment
of GnRH neurons during the prepubertal, or juvenile,
period of development, with that found in the adult.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Animals
Male rhesus monkeys born at the Primate Research
Laboratory (PRL), Center for Research in Reproductive
Physiology, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine,
were used in the study. Rooms within the PRL were
maintained on a photoperiodic regimen of 12 light:12 dark
(lights on at 0700 AM) and at a mean ambient temperature
of 20°C. Infant monkeys remained with their mother for
the ﬁrst 6 months of life, at which time they were
separated from the mothers and transferred to indoor pens
at the Primate Research Facility (PRF) where they were
housed with age matched peers in groups of 5–7. Artiﬁcial
photoperiod at the PRF was supplemented with natural
daylight. At approximately 1 year of age, the male mon-
keys were returned to the PRL where they were housed in
individual cages until the end of the experiment. After 6
months of age, the monkeys were fed once a day (Purina
Monkey Chow, Ralston Purina, St. Louis, MO, supple-
mented with fruit) and water was provided ad libitum.
Monkeys were maintained in accordance with NIH guide-
lines for the care and use of experimental animals, and the
experimental protocols were approved by the University of
Pittsburgh Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee.
Hormone radioimmunoassay
Circulating levels of LH were measured by using a
cynomolgus LH:anti-human chorionic gonadotropin (rab-
bit 13 pool D) radioimmunoassay system that employs the
rhesus pituitary LH preparation, WP-XV-20 (NICHHD
rhLH, RP-1), as standard (Peckham and Tontala, 1981).
The average minimal detectable dose was 1.1 ng NICHHD
RP-1/tube, and 100 µl aliquots of plasma were assayed.
The inter- and mean intra-assay coefficient of variation
was 9.6% and 2.5%, respectively.
Perfusion and preparation of tissue
for immunocytochemistry
Animals were sedated with ketamine hydrochloride (50
mg/animal, im; Ketoset, Aveco Co. Inc., Fort Dodge, IA)
and then deeply anesthetized with Na pentobarbital (ap-
proximately 25 mg/kg, i.v.; Nembutal, Abbott Laborato-
ries, North Chicago, IL). Immediately thereafter, the ani-
mals were thoracotomized, the descending aorta clamped,
and the head perfused via the ascending aorta with a
ﬁxative composed of 3% paraformaldehyde, 0.12% glutaral-
dehyde, 5% sucrose, and 0.9% NaCl in a 0.1 M Na
cacodylate buffer at pH 7.4. The ﬁxative was infused at a
rate of approximately 35 ml/minute by using a peristaltic
pump. Approximately 700 and 900 ml of ﬁxative was used
for the perfusion of the juvenile and adult brains, respec-
tively. Following perfusion, brains were removed and a
hypothalamic block, which extended in the rostral-caudal
direction from the preoptic area (POA) and organum
vasculosum of the lamina terminalis (OVLT) to the corpus
mammilare, was prepared and post-ﬁxed overnight at 4°C
in the ﬁxative minus glutaraldehyde. The following day,
the hypothalamic block was embedded in 2.4% agar and 40
µm coronal sections were cut on a Vibratome. The sections
were then washed overnight in 0.1 M Tris buffered saline
(TBS, pH 7.3) at 4°C to remove excess ﬁxative and
subsequently processed for immunocytochemistry.
Immunocytochemistry
The 40 µm sections were stained for GnRH by using a
modiﬁed version of a protocol previously described by
Goldsmith et al. (1990). Brieﬂy, sections were treated with
a 0.2 M solution of L-lysine for 30 minutes, washed in Tris
buffered saline (TBS), and placed in a series of alcohol
dilutions containing 10, 20, 40, 20, and 10% ethanol
(diluted in TBS). Sections were then rinsed several times
in TBS over 1 hour, before being placed for 20 minutes in a
mixture of 0.1% saponin, 0.01% Na azide, and 3% H2O2.
After washing in TBS for 1 hour, the sections were placed
in 3% normal goat serum (NGS) for 30 minutes. Sections
were subsequently washed in TBS over an hour and placed
in anti-GnRH antibody, LR1, at 1:10,000 in 0.1% NGS for
72 hours on a shaker at 4°C. Following incubation in the
primary antibody, the sections were rinsed in several
changes of TBS over a 4 hour period, placed in biotinylated
goat anti-rabbit IgG (1:200 in TBS) for 1 hour, and washed
in TBS. Sections were then placed for an additional hour in
avidin-biotin-horseradish peroxidase complex (ABC-HRP;
Vectorstain, Vector Laboratories, Burlingame, CA), which
was prepared by adding 10 µl each of avidin and biotinyl-
ated-HRP/ml of TBS. Following rinsing in ice cold 0.1 M
72
A.D. PERERA AND T.M. PLANT

Ultrastructural Studies of Neuronal

(GnRH) Release in the Rhesus Monkey

Department of Cell Biology and Physiology,

is present, it would be correspondingly most striking in the

nocytochemistry and electron microscopy (EM) was per-
formed to compare synaptic input and glial ensheathment
of GnRH neurons during the prepubertal, or juvenile,
period of development, with that found in the adult.
Male rhesus monkeys born at the Primate Research
Laboratory (PRL), Center for Research in Reproductive
Physiology, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine,
were used in the study. Rooms within the PRL were
maintained on a photoperiodic regimen of 12 light:12 dark
(lights on at 0700 AM) and at a mean ambient temperature
of 20°C. Infant monkeys remained with their mother for
the ﬁrst 6 months of life, at which time they were
separated from the mothers and transferred to indoor pens
at the Primate Research Facility (PRF) where they were
housed with age matched peers in groups of 5–7. Artiﬁcial
photoperiod at the PRF was supplemented with natural
daylight. At approximately 1 year of age, the male mon-
keys were returned to the PRL where they were housed in
individual cages until the end of the experiment. After 6
months of age, the monkeys were fed once a day (Purina
Monkey Chow, Ralston Purina, St. Louis, MO, supple-
mented with fruit) and water was provided ad libitum.
Monkeys were maintained in accordance with NIH guide-
lines for the care and use of experimental animals, and the
experimental protocols were approved by the University of
Pittsburgh Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee.
Circulating levels of LH were measured by using a
cynomolgus LH:anti-human chorionic gonadotropin (rab-
bit 13 pool D) radioimmunoassay system that employs the
rhesus pituitary LH preparation, WP-XV-20 (NICHHD
rhLH, RP-1), as standard (Peckham and Tontala, 1981).
The average minimal detectable dose was 1.1 ng NICHHD
RP-1/tube, and 100 µl aliquots of plasma were assayed.
The inter- and mean intra-assay coefficient of variation
was 9.6% and 2.5%, respectively.
Perfusion and preparation of tissue
Animals were sedated with ketamine hydrochloride (50
mg/animal, im; Ketoset, Aveco Co. Inc., Fort Dodge, IA)
and then deeply anesthetized with Na pentobarbital (ap-
proximately 25 mg/kg, i.v.; Nembutal, Abbott Laborato-
ries, North Chicago, IL). Immediately thereafter, the ani-
mals were thoracotomized, the descending aorta clamped,
and the head perfused via the ascending aorta with a
ﬁxative composed of 3% paraformaldehyde, 0.12% glutaral-
dehyde, 5% sucrose, and 0.9% NaCl in a 0.1 M Na
cacodylate buffer at pH 7.4. The ﬁxative was infused at a
rate of approximately 35 ml/minute by using a peristaltic
pump. Approximately 700 and 900 ml of ﬁxative was used
for the perfusion of the juvenile and adult brains, respec-
tively. Following perfusion, brains were removed and a
hypothalamic block, which extended in the rostral-caudal
direction from the preoptic area (POA) and organum
vasculosum of the lamina terminalis (OVLT) to the corpus
mammilare, was prepared and post-ﬁxed overnight at 4°C
in the ﬁxative minus glutaraldehyde. The following day,
the hypothalamic block was embedded in 2.4% agar and 40
µm coronal sections were cut on a Vibratome. The sections
were then washed overnight in 0.1 M Tris buffered saline
(TBS, pH 7.3) at 4°C to remove excess ﬁxative and
subsequently processed for immunocytochemistry.
The 40 µm sections were stained for GnRH by using a
modiﬁed version of a protocol previously described by
Goldsmith et al. (1990). Brieﬂy, sections were treated with
a 0.2 M solution of L-lysine for 30 minutes, washed in Tris
buffered saline (TBS), and placed in a series of alcohol
dilutions containing 10, 20, 40, 20, and 10% ethanol
(diluted in TBS). Sections were then rinsed several times
in TBS over 1 hour, before being placed for 20 minutes in a
mixture of 0.1% saponin, 0.01% Na azide, and 3% H2O2.
After washing in TBS for 1 hour, the sections were placed
in 3% normal goat serum (NGS) for 30 minutes. Sections
were subsequently washed in TBS over an hour and placed
in anti-GnRH antibody, LR1, at 1:10,000 in 0.1% NGS for
72 hours on a shaker at 4°C. Following incubation in the
primary antibody, the sections were rinsed in several
changes of TBS over a 4 hour period, placed in biotinylated
goat anti-rabbit IgG (1:200 in TBS) for 1 hour, and washed
in TBS. Sections were then placed for an additional hour in
avidin-biotin-horseradish peroxidase complex (ABC-HRP;
Vectorstain, Vector Laboratories, Burlingame, CA), which
was prepared by adding 10 µl each of avidin and biotinyl-
ated-HRP/ml of TBS. Following rinsing in ice cold 0.1 M
Tris buffer (TB, pH 7.4), the GnRH-antibody-peroxidase
complex was stained brown with a solution of TB contain-
ing 0.08% 3,38 diaminobenzidine (DAB), 0.14% imizadole,
and 0.002% H2O2. The reaction was stopped by placing the
sections in 0.1 M phosphate buffer.
Areas of 40 µm sections containing immunocytochemi-
cally labeled GnRH neurons were cut out and processed for
EM. For this purpose, moderately stained GnRH neurons
were chosen in order to maximize the detection of postsyn-
aptic structures. Tissue for EM was osmicated in 0.5%
OsO4 for 30 minutes, dehydrated in graded alcohol, placed
in propylene oxide, and ﬂat embedded in Epon between
two Teﬂon coated glass slides. The Epon was then cured for
48 hours. The glass slides were then prized apart and the
areas of tissue containing GnRH neurons were located, cut
out, and glued onto a pre-prepared epon block. Sequential
semi-thin sections (1 µm) were cut with a microtome and
sequentially examined under a light microscope until
brown reaction product was detected within a cell body. At
this point, ultrathin sections (60 nm) were cut and serially
collected on Cu thin bar grids or on alternate Cu and Ni
thin bar grids. The grids were then stained with 4% uranyl
acetate and Reynold lead citrate and examined and photo-
graphed on either a Joel B electron microscope at a
magniﬁcation of 8,000X or a Joel CX microscope at a
magniﬁcation 7,200X. Negatives were printed at a ﬁnal
magniﬁcation of 24,000X or 21,000X and the images
pasted together to form montages.
Linear distances and areas were measured from the
photographic montages by using a morphometric program
(Bioquant IV, R and M Biometrics, Inc., Nashville, TN)
attached to a digitized pad and connected to a Gateway
The search for a pubertal remodeling of synaptic input
and glial ensheathment of GnRH neurons was conducted
in agonadal monkeys. The rationale for this approach is as
follows. That the neurobiological mechanisms responsible
for the pubertal reaugmentation of pulsatile GnRH release
are brought into play in the absence of gonadal steroids is
demonstrated by the ﬁnding that, in the monkey, this
developmental change in neuroendocrine activity of the
hypothalamus is not interrupted by castration (Dierschke
et al., 1974; Plant, 1985). Thus, it is reasonable to propose
that, if hypothalamic plasticity indeed underlies the onset
of puberty, the same fundamental structural changes will
be observed in the agonadal animal at an age that puberty
would have occurred had the animal remained intact. In
the absence of the ovary or testis, however, any secondary
or tertiary structural changes that occur in intact animals
as a result of activation of gonadal steroid secretion will be
absent, allowing the fundamental hypothalamic remodel-
ling that is hypothesized to underlie increased GnRH
release to be described. To this end, eight male rhesus
monkeys were bilaterally castrated between the ages of
59–72 weeks, an age approximately midway through the
24 month period of prepubertal development when pulsa-
tile GnRH release is attenuated (Plant, 1985). Orchidec-
tomy was performed using aseptic technique after the
animals had been sedated with ketamine hydrochloride
(50 mg, i.m.) and anesthetized with Na pentobarbital
(approximately 25 mg/kg i.v. plus supplemental doses of 5
mg/kg as required). Four of the animals (the juvenile
group) were killed one month following gonadectomy (mean
age 71 6 1.9 weeks; mean BW 2.5 6 0.2 kg) and their
brains perfused. In the remaining animals (the adult
group), the ontogeny of pulsatile GnRH release was indi-
rectly tracked following castration by monitoring circulat-
ing LH concentrations every other week. When the ani-
mals’weight exceeded 3.0 kg, this procedure was performed
under ketamine sedation (50 mg/animal, i.m.). Following
the onset of the pubertal increase in pulsatile GnRH
release, as reﬂected by a rise in circulating LH concentra-
tions (Fig. 1), the animals in the adult group were killed
(mean age 145.2 6 4.6 weeks; mean BW 3.9 6 0.1 kg) and
GnRH neurons from two distinct hypothalamic regions,
namely the MBH and the region of the OVLT, were chosen
for analysis. That plasticity may selectively reside in the
neurons located within the MBH is suggested by ﬁndings
that the surgical isolation of the MBH in the prepubertal
female monkey does not appear to disrupt the subsequent
timing of the onset of puberty (Krey et al., 1981; Norman
and Spies, 1981), and that this hypothalamic region is rich
in PSA-NCAM (Perera et al., 1993) and contains a rela-
tively high percentage of neuroendocrine GnRH neurons
(Goldsmith et al., 1990). The analysis of GnRH neurons
within the region of the OVLT of the hypothalamus was
undertaken in order to provide information for an area
where plastic changes in association with puberty would
be predicted perhaps not to occur.
In the present study, the percentage of membrane
occupied by synaptic density was used as an index of
synaptic input. Although stereological methods, such as
dissector, for obtaining unbiased counts of synaptic num-
ber have been recently introduced (see Coggeshall and
Lekan, 1996) such procedures are not readily applicable to
analysis of rare events. In the case of the GnRH neuronal
network, perikarya are diffusely distributed throughout
the hypothalamus (Silverman et al., 1994) necessitating
that individual cell bodies be identiﬁed by pre-embedding
staining and ﬁrst removed from thick sections before
processing for ultrastructural analysis. Moreover, GnRH
neurons are scantly innervated (Silverman et al., 1994)
and, therefore, the chances of ﬁnding, in the two sequen-
tial ultrathin sections employed in dissector, the last
proﬁle (a count denoted by Q-in the stereological litera-
ture, see Coggeshall and Lekan, 1996) of a synapse affer-
ent to such an isolated neuron, is remote. Thus, in the case
of the GnRH neuron, an unbiased analysis of synaptic
number, which may only be performed using the dissector
principle or by serial reconstruction, becomes prohibitively
labor intensive. Since, moreover, an estimate of absolute
synaptic number was not deemed necessary because of the
comparative nature of this study, the percentage of mem-
brane occupied by synaptic density was used as an index of
synaptic input; an approach taken in previous studies of
similar problems (Witkin et al, 1991; Witkin, 1992; Witkin
Accordingly, three to ﬁve neurons from each of the two
brain regions from each monkey were analyzed. The
rational for selecting a few neurons from a relatively large
number of animals was to provide representative popula-
tions of cells in each developmental group. Thirty-seven
(18 MBH:19 OVLT) GnRH neurons in the juvenile group
NEURONAL PLASTICITY AND PRIMATE PUBERTY
and 40 (20 MBH:20 OVLT) GnRH neurons in the adult
group were examined. For the analysis of each GnRH
perikaryon, nine cross-sectional proﬁles, separated by
180–300 nm, were photographed. This protocol was adopted
in order to sample a relatively large fraction of the surface
area of the GnRH perikaryon. The variation in the interval
of sampling for the ultra-thin sections resulted from a
combination of a loss of sections and an occasional GnRH
proﬁle falling on the crosswire of a grid. In those GnRH
neurons that were sectioned in the longitudinal plane and
through the nucleus, perikaryal size was ascertained by
measuring the area of a single randomly chosen nucleated
proﬁle. It was reasoned that such a proﬁle should reﬂect
the maximal cross sectional area, since observations in the
monkey at the light microscopic level indicate that c-fos
stained nuclei are centrally located in these cells (Hoffman
and Perera, unpublished observations). Nucleolar size was
estimated for each GnRH neuron examined by measuring
the area of a randomly chosen cross-section taken through
the nucleolus. Random selection was achieved in both of
the above instances by assigning the respective proﬁles
numbers and then blindly choosing a number. The length
of post-synaptic density on the plasma membrane of GnRH
neurons were measured. In the rare cases where synapses
were cut en face, and it was not possible to distinguish the
pre- and post-synaptic densities, the length of the total
density was measured. Synaptic density lengths were
estimated separately for the GnRH cell body and dendrite
in order to assess whether these two components of the
GnRH neuron were differentially regulated. The fusiform
shape of most GnRH neurons and the often heavily
invaginated nuclei, make it difficult to create a simple
algorithm (i.e., distance from nucleus) to objectively iden-
tify the demarcation between perikaryon and dendrite. In
addition, not all neuronal proﬁles bearing dendrites con-
tained nuclei. Therefore, the origin of dendrites was arbi-
trarily identiﬁed as immediately distal to the region of the
cell body that tapered most acutely. For the cell body, the
lengths of individual synaptic densities were measured on
all nine sections, summed and expressed as a percentage of
total plasma membrane analyzed for a given cell. Although
dendrites were usually observed in six or seven cross-
sections per neuron, the length of dendritic membrane
sampled was highly variable. For this reason, the synaptic
density lengths were summed for a given monkey and
expressed as a percentage of the total dendritic plasma
membrane analyzed for that animal. Attempts were made
to identify the qualitative nature of both axosomatic and
axodendritic synapses by noting whether the shape of the
vesicles within the presynaptic element were round or
Lastly, the length of plasma membrane of the GnRH
perikaryon, and that of the dendrite when present, which
was directly juxtaposed to an astroglial process was mea-
sured and, as for synaptic density, expressed as a percent-
age of the total plasma membrane analyzed. For the cell
body, glial juxtaposition was assessed for each neuron,
while total dendritic glial ensheathment was determined
for each animal. The presence of ﬁbrils was used to
establish the identity of glial processes (Fig. 2).
An estimation of differences in synaptic innervation
between GnRH and non-GnRH neurons was made by
comparing the percentage length of plasma membrane
that exhibited a postsynaptic density in a single neuronal
proﬁle of a GnRH neuron with that of its nearest non-
GnRH neighbor. This comparison was performed on 45
Semi-schematic time course of circulating plasma luteiniz-
ing hormone (LH) concentrations, an endocrine indice of pulsatile
gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) activity, in male monkeys
studied as adults (n 5 4). Hatched arrowheads indicate the ages at
which these animals were killed. For comparison, the ages at which
juvenile monkeys (n 5 4) were perfused are also indicated (closed
arrowheads). All monkeys were castrated between 59 to 72 weeks of
pairs of neurons from ﬁve animals, and for this purpose
neurons from the two developmental states and the two
hypothalamic areas were pooled.
For those parameters which were derived from observa-
tions on three to ﬁve GnRH neurons per monkey, a mean
value was obtained for each animal by averaging the
individual values obtained for that animal. The averaged
values (n 5 4, for each group) were then used for assessing
the signiﬁcance of differences between the juvenile and
adult groups. An unpaired two-tailed t-test was used when
the variance between the groups was homogenous. If this
homogeneity of variance assumption was violated, a Mann-
Whitney U-test was used. A similar approach was used to
compare differences in synaptic density between GnRH
and non-GnRH cells, and for the analysis of synaptic
density and glial ensheathment of GnRH dendrites. In the
case of the later parameter, however, only one observation
per animal was available (see above). The level of signiﬁ-
cance for all tests was set at P , 0.05.
GnRH neurons were identiﬁed at the ultrastructural
level by the presence of osmium enhanced DAB reaction
product within the cytoplasm (Figs. 2–4). In some in-
stances, the chromogen was also located within the nucleus
(Figs. 3, 4). The GnRH neurons were generally fusiform in
shape and the perikaryal plasma membrane was rela-
tively smooth in appearance (Figs. 3, 4). Occasionally, an
irregularly contoured or ‘‘spiny’’ cell body was located in
the adult group (2% of cells, not shown). Endoplasmic
reticulum and Golgi were present throughout the cyto-
plasm of all GnRH cell bodies. A solitary cilium was
detected in a single neuron from the region of the OVLT of
an adult animal (not shown).All processes emanating from
GnRH perikarya were, without exception, dendritic in
nature (e.g., Fig. 3). In the MBH, the average length of
dendritic membrane per GnRH neuron observed in adults
was signiﬁcantly greater (t-test) than that found in juve-
niles, and this was related to a greater proportion of
bipolar GnRH neurons in this region of the hypothalamus

in the adult group (Fig. 5). This developmental difference
in GnRH dendritic conﬁguration was not observed in the
Seventy-six of the 77 neurons analyzed were sectioned
in the longitudinal plane and through the nucleus. The
mean cross-sectional areas of GnRH perikarya in juveniles
and adults in both the MBH and the region of the OVLT
(Table 1) were not statistically signiﬁcant (Mann-Whitney
U-test). Similarly, nucleolar volumes of GnRH neurons in
juveniles and adults were not signiﬁcantly different (Mann-
Whitney U-test) in either region of the hypothalamus
Synaptic innervation of GnRH neurons
The percentage of plasma membrane of GnRH perikarya
in the MBH and the region of the OVLT occupied by
synaptic density in individual juvenile and adult monkeys
is shown in Table 2. The percentage of GnRH perikaryal
membrane in the MBH occupied by synaptic density
(0.207 6 0.073%, mean 6 SEM, n 5 4) was signiﬁcantly
greater (Mann-Whitney U-test) than that in adults (0.047 6
0.006%, n 5 4). In the juvenile group, more than 27% of
GnRH cell bodies in the MBH had greater than 0.3% of
their plasma membrane occupied by a synaptic density,
whereas, in adults, none of the mediobasal hypothalamic
GnRH cells were innervated to this degree (Fig. 6, upper
In the case of the OVLT, there was no signiﬁcant
difference (t-test and Mann-Whitney U-test) between juve-
niles and adults in the degree to which the plasma
membrane of GnRH perikarya was occupied by synaptic
density (Table 2 and Fig. 6, lower panel).
Forty-one percent of synapses on GnRH cell bodies in
the juvenile MBH were categorized as pleiomorphic in
nature, and this compared to a value of 64% in the adult
MBH. In the region of the OVLT, analogous values for this
parameter were 43%, and 13% for the juvenile and adult
groups, respectively. It should be noted, however, that in
many cases a decision on whether to assign vesicular
shape within a given synapse as pleiomorphic or round
was difficult to reach. For this reason, the percentage of
the perikaryal membrane occupied by these two synaptic
types was not subjected to statistical analysis.
An examination of synaptic innervation of GnRH den-
drites failed to reveal signiﬁcant differences (Mann-
Whitney U-test) between juveniles and adults for cells
found either in the MBH or in the region of the OVLT (data
not shown). Sixty percent of synapses on GnRH dendrites
in the juvenile MBH contained pleiomorphic vesicles com-
pared to 30% in the adult MBH. In the OVLT region, 33%
of the GnRH dendrites in the juvenile contained pleiomor-
phic vesicles compared to 50% in the adult.
Without exception, GnRH immunoreactivity was not
found in the presynaptic element of contacts on either the
cell body or the dendrite(s) of GnRH neurons examined in

neurons, and this difference was signiﬁcant (Mann-

were 24.6 6 6.3% and 35.9 6 3.5%, respectively. Although
no statistically signiﬁcant developmental changes in this
parameter were observed, the degree of glial ensheath-

and organum vasculosum of the lamina terminalis (OVLT; right) from

Developmental differences in glial coverage of GnRH
dendrites were not detected in either hypothalamic area.
Puberty in primates is occasioned by an activation of the
pituitary-gonadal axis, which, in turn, is triggered by an
increase in pulsatile release of the hypothalamic peptide,
GnRH, into the hypophysial portal circulation. The pre-
sent ultrastructural study of the rhesus monkey examined
the hypothesis that the pubertal augmentation of GnRH
release results from a remodeling of synaptic and/or glial
inputs to the hypothalamic neurons that synthesize and
secrete this neuropeptide. The gross morphological fea-
tures of GnRH perikarya in both the MBH and the region
of the OVLT taken several months before the pubertal
increase in GnRH release was anticipated were similar to
those from monkeys perfused 1 to 2 months after the
initiation of this neuroendocrine event. Although, Cam-
eron et al. (1985) reported that GnRH perikaryal size in
intact crab-eating monkeys is larger in adult than in
juvenile animals, GnRH gene expression, as reﬂected by
hypothalamic content of this peptide (Fraser et al., 1989)
and in situ mRNA levels (Vician et al., 1991), is not
upregulated at puberty. In the present study, an occasional
‘‘spiny’’ cell was observed in the adult, but GnRH peri-
karya, in general, were fairly uniform in the two develop-
mental stages examined. Thus, it is unlikely that such
topographical changes are related to initiation of the
pubertal mode of GnRH secretion in primates, as sug-
gested for non-primate species (Wray and Hoffman, 1985).
Support for the view that synaptic plasticity may play
an important role in the pubertal increase of GnRH release
is provided by the ﬁnding that, in the absence of signiﬁcant
changes in GnRH cell body size, synaptic density on the
perikaryal membranes of these neurons in MBH of juve-
niles was signiﬁcantly greater than that in the adult
group. Although the time course of the remodeling of
synaptic input to GnRH neurons that must have occurred
between these two stages of development was not studied,
it is reasonable to conclude that it occurred concomitantly
with initiation of the pubertal mode of GnRH release.

NEURONAL PLASTICITY AND PRIMATE PUBERTY
Since the present experiment was conducted in agonadal
monkeys, any decrease in synaptic density observed follow-
ing the onset of the pubertal increase in GnRH release
could not have been produced by circulating testicular
steroids, which in the intact animal increase at this
developmental stage (Plant, 1994). The possibility that
adrenal sex steroids play a role in hypothalamic remodel-
ing may also be rejected because in macaques, in contrast
to hominoids, adrenarche, which is characterized by in-
creased adrenal androgen secretion, is not observed (Cut-
ler et al., 1978; Smail et al., 1982).
In contrast to the pubertal remodeling of synaptic input
to GnRH perikarya in the MBH, developmental changes in
synaptic density on the perikarya of these neurons in the
region of the OVLT were not observed. In this regard, it
may be noted that Witkin et al. (1995) concluded from two
independent studies of gonad intact female rhesus mon-
keys that density of synaptic input to GnRH neurons in the
preoptic area was not different in pubertal and adult
animals. Taking the foregoing considerations together
with the ﬁnding of others that the onset of puberty in the
monkey is not interrupted following surgical interruption
of GnRH projections from the OVLT to the median emi-
nence (Krey et al., 1981; Norman and Spies, 1981), it may
be suggested that the remodeling in the MBH is causally
related to the pubertal increase in GnRH release. It is to be
noted that the foregoing conclusion is at variance with that
proposed in a preliminary report of this study (Perera and
Plant, 1994). In the initial publication, however, no at-
tempt was made distinguish between GnRH neurons in
the MBH and those in the region of the OVLT. Moreover,
an additional monkey was added to each development
group in the completed study reported here.
Although the developmental decline in synaptic density
on GnRH perikarya in the MBH seems moderate, it should
be noted that, as previously reported by Silverman et al.
(1994), these cells were found to be scantly innervated.
Moreover, not all these neurons are hypophysiotropic in
nature (Goldsmith et al., 1990), and therefore inclusion in
the analysis of any GnRH cells that did not project to the
median eminence may have diluted the magnitude of the
While our ﬁndings indicate that the increase in pulsatile
GnRH release at puberty may be activated by the with-
drawal of an inhibitory input to the GnRH neurons in the
MBH, attempts to reinforce this notion by classifying
synapses on the basis of vesicular topology was unsuccess-
ful. Gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) is the major inhibi-
tory neurotransmitter in the brain, and GABAergic syn-
apses have been demonstrated on GnRH neurons in rat
(Leranth et al., 1985a). Although a similar association has
yet to be demonstrated in the primate hypothalamus,
GABA may provide the putative inhibitory tone to the
GnRH neuron during prepubertal development in the
monkey. In the female, hypothalamic release of this amino
acid declines with the onset of puberty and local hypotha-
lamic administration of the GABAA receptor antagonist,
bicuculline, elicits GnRH release in prepubertal animals
(Mitsushima et al., 1994). Although preferential location of
GABA containing boutons on perikarya was not observed
in a recent study of the rat hypothalamus (Decavel and
van den Pol, 1990), it is generally recognized that synapses
on cell bodies are often inhibitory (Kandel and Schwartz,
1991). Moreover, because of the proximity of such synapses
to the axon hillock they are therefore more efficacious than
those on dendrites (Shepherd, 1979). This dogma is cer-
tainly congruent with the proposal that the pubertal
increase in GnRH release may be associated with a modest
loss of GABAergic input to GnRH perikarya. Double-
labeling studies, of course, are necessary to deﬁnitively
identify the neurochemical phenotype of the synaptic
density that is lost at the time of puberty in the monkey.
In the rat, GnRH itself has been implicated as an
inhibitory component of a autoregulatory feedback loop
that controls the developmental release of this neuropep-
tide (Bourguignon et al., 1990), and in this species axo-
somatic and axo-dendritic GnRH-GnRH synapses have
been described (Leranth et al., 1985b; Witkin and Silver-
man, 1985). The absence of such contacts in the present
study was unexpected because autoregulatory GnRH syn-
apses have also been reported in an infant female cynomol-
gus monkey (Thind and Goldsmith, 1988). While the
Degree of glial ensheathment of GnRH perikarya of juvenile
(solid bars) and adult (hatched bars) male monkeys as reﬂected by the
frequency distribution of GnRH cell bodies in the MBH (top) and
OVLT (bottom) with increasing percentages of their perikaryal
membrane directly juxtaposed to the glial membrane.
reason for this difference is unclear, we must conclude that
synaptically mediated autoregulation probably does not
play a role in controlling the ontogeny of GnRH release in
the rhesus monkey. This conclusion is supported by the
ﬁndings that peripheral and intracerebroventricular ad-
ministration of GnRH receptor agonists and antagonists
fail to alter electrophysiological correlates of pulsatile
GnRH release in adult monkeys (Kesner et al., 1986;
That pubertal ampliﬁcation of GnRH release may result
from the appearance of an excitatory signal, rather than
loss of an inhibitory cue has also been proposed (Perera
and Plant, 1992). This notion is based, in part, on the
ﬁnding that repetitive administration of the glutamate
receptor agonist, N-methyl-D-aspartate, results in a con-
comitant and sustained train of GnRH discharges in the
juvenile monkey (Gay and Plant, 1988). The recent ultra-
structural demonstration of glutamate synapses on GnRH
neurons in the MBH of the rhesus monkey (Goldsmith et
al., 1994) provides an anatomical basis for a role of this
excitatory amino acid in the control of puberty. For the
foregoing reasons, it is therefore tempting to speculate
that our observation of a greater dendritic length on GnRH
neurons in the adult MBH may perhaps provide an
anatomical substrate for a heightened excitatory tone to
these neuroendocrine cells without an increase in synaptic
density. In this regard, it should be noted that developmen-
tal changes in the dendritic polarity of GnRH neurons
have been reported for the hamster (Yellon and Newman,
An examination of glial ensheathment of GnRH neurons
was prompted by the growing evidence that these cells
play an active role in neuroendocrine neuronal function
(Hatton, 1988; Theodosis and Poulain, 1992), and by the
observation that in the female monkey alterations in
gonadal status result in changes in glial ensheathment of
GnRH neurons (Witkin et al., 1991). Glial cells may effect
neuronal function either by providing a barrier to synapse
formation, or by producing paracrine factors that directly
effect neuronal activity. In regard to the latter, Ma et al.
(1994) have recently demonstrated that, in the female
monkey, hypothalamic astroglial expression of transform-
ing growth factor alpha, a peptide shown to stimulate
GnRH release in the rat (Ojeda et al., 1990) is greater in
adult than in juvenile animals. Since, in the present study,
developmental changes in glial ensheathment of GnRH
neurons were not observed within the MBH, the trigger for
initiation of the pubertal mode of GnRH release in pri-
mates does not appear to involve major restructuring
between glial cells and GnRH neurons. When evaluating
the validity of this conclusion, two caveats must be borne
in mind. Firstly, in our hands, quantitative assessment of
glial ensheathment was difficult, and it is conceivable that
very ﬁne glial processes may have escaped identiﬁcation.
Secondly, in addition to the present suggestion of a develop-
mental increase in glial ensheathment on GnRH perikarya
within the region of the OVLT, developmental changes in
this parameter within the preoptic area of the gonad intact
female rhesus monkey have been proposed (Witkin et al.,
1995). While current understanding indicates that the
control system governing the onset of primate puberty
resides within the MBH, changes in glial ensheathment of
GnRH neurons in more anterior regions of the hypothala-
mus perhaps require additional study.
In summary, the ﬁnding in the rhesus monkey that
synaptic innervation of GnRH cell bodies in the MBH
declines during the transition from the apulsatile prepuber-
tal mode of GnRH release to an intermittent pubertal
pattern of discharge provides the ﬁrst systematic demon-
stration of a neuroanatomical basis for the onset of pu-
berty in primates. Further, the data support the view that
this fundamental developmental event that underlies all
aspects of sexual maturation is the result of plasticity
within the GnRH neuronal network. Double labeling stud-
ies are now required to identify the neurotransmitter
phenotype of the synaptic density on mediobasal hypotha-
lamic GnRH perikarya that is attenuated at the onset of
